summary: Significant high-risk regulatory changes impact food seasoning/snack compliance. **Codex** now requires verification of contaminant limits (mycotoxins, metals). The **European Union** mandates new microbiology limits for foodborne bacteria (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria, including shelf-life studies for RTE foods) and production hygiene. **GCC/GSO** introduces new allergen declaration, mandatory expiration dates, and microbiological limits. **Singapore** enforces strict compliance for food additive and contaminant limits, prohibiting non-approved additives or products exceeding contaminant thresholds, and requires full ingredient/additive declaration. **Taiwan** now mandates specific allergen declarations, including sulphite. The **United States** has added prohibited additive substances, defined nine major food allergens, set new contaminant guidance/action levels (3-MCPD, Aflatoxins), and implemented detailed labeling rules for allergens (e.g., 'contains' statements, specific types), Cochineal extract/carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, and sulfiting agents, plus a 'gluten-free' standard. Minor high-risk modifications occurred in **GCC** and **Thailand** for general labelling and prohibited food products. Moderate-risk changes include numerous additions across China, EU, GCC/GSO, Indonesia, and Japan (additive, contaminant, microbiology, labelling), alongside several removals from Codex, EU, GCC, India, and Japan, which require verification.

changes.parameter: Contaminants (e.g., mycotoxins, metals like arsenic, lead, mercury)
changes.newLimit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995)
changes.notes: The Codex Alimentarius Commission establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants to be legally permitted in food or feed commodities to protect human health. Specific MLs are detailed in standards such as CXS 193-1995.

changes.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
changes.newLimit: Verification required from Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005
changes.notes: Food products placed on the market must comply with food safety criteria.

changes.newLimit: Compliance with food safety criteria defined in Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005, requiring shelf-life studies and sampling procedures
changes.notes: Mandatory procedures (shelf-life studies, sampling) for food business operators manufacturing RTE food to manage L. monocytogenes risk.

changes.newLimit: Verification required from Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005
changes.notes: Food production processes must comply with process hygiene criteria.
Specific requirements for allergen declaration on pre-packaged foodstuffs must be verified by consulting the full standard.
Specific labelling requirements must be verified by consulting the full technical regulation.
Specific mandatory expiration date requirements must be verified by consulting the full technical regulation.
Specific microbiological limits must be verified by consulting the full technical regulation.
All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

changes.newLimit: Must have technological justification and not present health risks
changes.notes: Food additives are allowed when there is a technological justification and they do not present health risks to consumers.

changes.newLimit: Do not import or sell, unless SFA approval
changes.notes: If a food product contains an additive that is not permitted, it must not be imported or sold, unless an application to SFA for evaluation is submitted.
All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents (contaminants) in food.

changes.newLimit: Must be within SFA's regulatory limits
changes.notes: Contaminants in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
Food products containing contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed regulatory limits must not be imported or sold.
Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of diethylstibestrol, hexoestrol, or dienoestrol.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared in list of ingredients
changes.notes: All ingredients and additives used in prepacked foods for sale in Singapore must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.

changes.newLimit: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
changes.notes: These allergens must be declared if present as an ingredient or food additive in prepackaged foods.
Sulphite must be declared if the residue sulphur dioxide is 10 mg/kg or more in prepackaged foods.

changes.newLimit: Foods listed as prohibited must not be produced, imported, or sold.
changes.notes: A list of foods prohibited from production, import, or sale exists and must be consulted.
Substances listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food are labeled 'PROHIBITED' or 'PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS'.
The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
This is a guidance level.
This is an action level.
Cochineal extract and carmine must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

changes.parameter: Declaration of Major Food Allergens (contains statement)
changes.notes: The allergen's food source must be declared immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.
The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear in parentheses following the name of the ingredient.
All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels.
A final rule defines 'gluten-free' for food labeling. Compliance requirements also exist for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the 'gluten-free' claim.

changes.parameter: Specificity in Major Food Allergen Labeling
changes.notes: The type of tree nut (e.g., almonds, pecans, walnuts), and the species of fish (e.g., bass, flounder, cod) and Crustacean shellfish (e.g., crab, lobster, shrimp) must be declared.
Sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
This standard specifies the varieties, scope of use, and maximum usage levels of food additives.

changes.newLimit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
changes.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods. Users are encouraged to consult the database and its Preamble for specific provisions.
A Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) exists for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices.
